tillage.1: Regular ploughing inverts and destroys soil structure, kills earthworms and fungal networks, releases stored carbon and creates compaction hardpan layers over time.
tillage.2: Minimal, targeted tillage reduces soil disturbance to a fraction of conventional practice, preserving most biological activity while allowing necessary interventions.
tillage.3: Permanent no-till keeps the soil food web intact, maintains mycorrhizal networks, and locks in organic matter — the foundation of long-term soil fertility.

soil_cover.1: Bare soil is exposed to erosion from rain and wind, UV degradation, extreme temperature swings and rain-impact compaction — all of which destroy soil structure and life.
soil_cover.2: Organic mulch or partial cover insulates the soil, adds organic matter as it decomposes, retains moisture and provides habitat for soil organisms.
soil_cover.3: Year-round living cover continuously feeds soil organisms through root exudates, prevents erosion, fixes nitrogen (with legumes) and suppresses weeds without chemicals.

soil_structure.1: Compacted soil has poor water infiltration, root penetration and gas exchange. It signals absent or severely stressed biological activity.
soil_structure.2: Moderate structure with visible aggregates indicates biological activity is recovering — a good sign of improving soil health.
soil_structure.3: Loose, crumbly soil with earthworms indicates a thriving soil ecosystem, excellent water infiltration and high biological activity — the goal of regenerative management.

irrigation.1: Flood irrigation can waste 40–60% of water through runoff and evaporation in Valencia's climate, accelerating aquifer depletion and risking soil salinisation.
irrigation.2: Localised drip reduces water use significantly compared to flood irrigation and begins to match water supply to plant needs.
irrigation.3: Sensor-guided precision drip delivers water only when soil moisture drops below the threshold plants need, minimising waste and plant stress simultaneously.

rainwater.1: Without any water retention, rainfall runs off the farm carrying topsoil and nutrients, while the farm remains entirely dependent on irrigation infrastructure.
rainwater.2: Passive water harvesting infrastructure such as terraces and swales slows runoff, increases infiltration and can significantly recharge local groundwater.
rainwater.3: Active rainwater storage creates on-farm water independence, reduces irrigation costs and provides resilience during Valencia's increasingly frequent drought periods.

water_source.1: Unmonitored deep aquifer extraction depletes shared groundwater resources that may take centuries to recharge, threatening the long-term viability of farming in Valencia.
water_source.2: Using a mix of sources within legal rights is a pragmatic transition step, reducing pressure on any single water body.
water_source.3: Relying primarily on surface water or rainfall minimises pressure on aquifers, supports the regional water balance and aligns with sustainable farming.

hedges.1: Bare or concrete boundaries eliminate habitat corridors for wildlife, leaving the farm as an ecological island disconnected from surrounding biodiversity.
hedges.2: Hedges and flower strips on farm boundaries provide nesting habitat, food sources and movement corridors for beneficial insects, birds and small mammals.
hedges.3: Multiple interconnected corridors linking to the wider landscape multiply ecological benefits, creating resilient populations of pollinators and natural pest predators.

pollinators.1: Without pollinator habitat, citrus relies on chance for pollination and lacks the natural predator populations that control pest outbreaks.
pollinators.2: A dedicated flower strip or insect refuge provides refuge and food for beneficial insects, improving both pollination rates and biological pest control.
pollinators.3: Multiple managed habitats create year-round resources for diverse pollinator and predator species, reducing reliance on chemical pest control and improving yields.

biodiversity_trend.1: A declining trend is a warning signal that the farming system is actively degrading the ecological foundation it depends on — this is urgent to address.
biodiversity_trend.2: A slight increase indicates that some practices are already moving in the right direction, even if the full ecosystem has not yet recovered.
biodiversity_trend.3: Observed increases in multiple species groups confirm that the farm's ecological management is working and building genuine resilience.

pesticides.1: Calendar-based synthetic spraying kills beneficial insects including pollinators and natural predators, creates pesticide resistance and contaminates soil and water.
pesticides.2: Replacing most synthetics with biological or organic products protects beneficial organisms, reduces environmental contamination and builds natural pest suppression.
pesticides.3: Full biological pest management through ecological balance eliminates pesticide costs, protects human health, preserves biodiversity and builds long-term resilience.

herbicides.1: Regular herbicides keep soil bare, destroy the understory plant community, eliminate habitat for beneficial insects and contaminate soil and groundwater.
herbicides.2: Mechanical management avoids chemical contamination. Eliminating herbicide use entirely is the regenerative goal.
herbicides.3: Replacing herbicides with cover crops or mulch eliminates chemical inputs, adds organic matter, provides habitat and can actually improve soil structure.

synthetic_fertiliser.1: Synthetic NPK fertilisers bypass the soil food web, acidify soils over time, contaminate groundwater through leaching and create dependency on external supply chains.
synthetic_fertiliser.2: Shifting primarily to organic inputs begins to rebuild soil biology and reduce chemical dependency while maintaining adequate nutrition.
synthetic_fertiliser.3: Fully organic biological inputs feed soil organisms rather than bypassing them, building long-term fertility, improving water retention and reducing costs over time.

organic_matter.1: Removing or burning organic residues depletes soil organic matter, reduces water retention, diminishes carbon sequestration and starves soil organisms.
organic_matter.2: Chipping prunings as mulch and applying compost returns nutrients and organic matter to the soil, gradually rebuilding the biological foundation.
organic_matter.3: A systematic programme of organic matter return maximises carbon input, feeds diverse soil organisms and builds soil organic matter — the foundation of fertility.

biofertilisers.1: Relying solely on mineral fertilisers feeds the plant but not the soil, leading to progressive decline of soil biology and increasing dependency on external inputs.
biofertilisers.2: Mycorrhizal inoculants and biostimulants reinforce the soil–plant symbiosis, improving nutrient uptake efficiency and plant resilience to stress.
biofertilisers.3: A regular biological programme actively builds the soil microbiome, increases nutrient cycling efficiency and reduces the need for external inputs over time.

cover_crops_nutrition.1: No cover crops leaves the soil biologically inactive between rows, losing the opportunity to fix nitrogen, build organic matter and support soil life.
cover_crops_nutrition.2: Including legumes in the cover crop mix allows biological nitrogen fixation, potentially supplying 30–150 kg N/ha/year without synthetic fertiliser.
cover_crops_nutrition.3: A diverse cover crop mix with legumes, grasses and native species creates maximum biodiversity above and below ground, optimising nitrogen fixation, water infiltration and soil structure.

cover_management.1: Eliminating all vegetation between trees removes habitat, food sources, soil cover and the biological control agents that keep pest populations in balance.
cover_management.2: Allowing vegetation to grow and cycle naturally with minimal mowing maintains soil cover, organic matter input and habitat for beneficial organisms year-round.
cover_management.3: Planned grazing integrates livestock into the system, converting biomass to fertility while controlling vegetation without machinery, fuel or herbicides.

grazing.1: No livestock integration misses the opportunity to convert on-farm biomass into fertility and manage vegetation without mechanical or chemical inputs.
grazing.2: Planned seasonal grazing with controlled timing prevents overgrazing, distributes fertility evenly and contributes to soil organic matter through manure.
grazing.3: Fully planned rotational grazing mimics natural grazing patterns, maximises soil biology stimulation, prevents compaction and integrates livestock as a farm asset.

pruning_waste.1: Burning prunings destroys organic matter, releases CO₂, kills soil-surface organisms and eliminates a valuable on-farm fertility resource.
pruning_waste.2: Chipping prunings and leaving them as mulch recycles nutrients back to the soil surface, protects from erosion and feeds soil organisms as they decompose.
pruning_waste.3: Composting or using chipped prunings as both mulch and animal feed creates a closed nutrient loop, maximising on-farm fertility and minimising external inputs.

intercropping.1: Monoculture creates uniform conditions that favour pest and disease outbreaks, requires high external inputs and offers no resilience if one crop fails.
intercropping.2: Integrating other species creates habitat niches, biological diversity and income diversification, beginning to move away from the fragility of monoculture.
intercropping.3: A diverse agroforestry system maximises ecological interactions, creates multiple income streams, provides habitat, sequesters more carbon and reduces systemic risk.

varieties.1: Reliance on a single commercial variety creates genetic uniformity — a new pest or pathogen could devastate the entire farm.
varieties.2: Multiple varieties including traditional ones provides genetic diversity, market diversification and resilience against climate variability.
varieties.3: A diverse mix of local, traditional and disease-resistant varieties adapted to Valencia's microclimate maximises resilience, ecological fit and market interest.

trap_crops.1: Without companion plants or trap crops, the only pest management tools are chemical ones, creating resistance, costs and ecological damage over time.
trap_crops.2: Targeted trap crops or companion plants for specific pests begin to use ecological processes for pest management, reducing chemical dependency.
trap_crops.3: A strategic companion planting system creates year-round ecological balance, attracting beneficial insects, confusing pests and providing habitat for natural predators.

plastics_use.1: Heavy single-use plastic use contributes to microplastic contamination of soil and water, creates disposal costs and is increasingly restricted by EU regulations.
plastics_use.2: Actively replacing single-use with reusable or biodegradable alternatives reduces contamination, costs and regulatory risk.
plastics_use.3: Minimal plastic use throughout the farm represents full alignment with circular economy principles and future EU agricultural sustainability requirements.

plastics_disposal.1: Burning agricultural plastics releases toxic compounds including dioxins and furans. Leaving them in fields causes progressive microplastic soil contamination.
plastics_disposal.2: Municipal waste disposal is the legal minimum but lacks the traceability and professional handling needed for proper agricultural plastic recycling.
plastics_disposal.3: Certified plastic management with documented traceability ensures proper recycling, protects from liability and demonstrates full compliance with regulations.

planning.1: Farming without a structured plan makes it very difficult to measure progress, identify priorities or demonstrate improvement to buyers or certifiers.
planning.2: A clear improvement plan enables systematic progress, better resource allocation and clearer communication with advisors and cooperative networks.
planning.3: A documented long-term plan with annual monitoring creates accountability, enables adaptive management and is often required for regenerative certification.

monitoring.1: Without systematic observation it is impossible to know if practices are working, to detect early warning signs or to demonstrate progress to buyers or researchers.
monitoring.2: Regular observation with records enables trend detection, captures seasonal variation and provides evidence for certifications or research partnerships.
monitoring.3: Systematic monitoring with quantitative data creates a rigorous evidence base, enables adaptive management decisions and is the foundation of continuous improvement.

training.1: Without active learning, farming practices tend to remain static while the science and market expectations for regenerative agriculture continue to evolve rapidly.
training.2: Regular courses and farm visits provide new techniques, peer learning and motivation — essential for staying current with regenerative practices.
training.3: Active participation in research networks and knowledge exchange accelerates learning, contributes to the wider field and builds connections that open market opportunities.

networks.1: Farming in isolation misses the peer learning, collective bargaining power and market access that cooperative and network membership provides.
networks.2: Active knowledge exchange within a cooperative or group multiplies learning opportunities and helps share the costs and risks of transition.
networks.3: Membership in a regenerative agriculture network connects you to the forefront of practice, research, policy and market access for certified regenerative products.

market.1: Selling exclusively to large distributors leaves the farmer price-exposed, with no connection to final consumers who might pay a premium for regenerative products.
market.2: Mixed sales channels begin to build direct relationships and test the premium market while maintaining the security of distributor volumes.
market.3: Significant local or direct sales connect the farmer to consumers who value quality and sustainability, enabling premium prices and feedback that drives further improvement.

sharing.1: Keeping practices private misses the opportunity to build reputation, attract research partnerships, access premium markets and contribute to collective regenerative knowledge.
sharing.2: Participating in farm visits or events builds relationships with peers, researchers and consumers — creating opportunities beyond the farm gate.
sharing.3: Active knowledge sharing through multiple channels builds strong reputation, attracts research attention, opens premium market access and contributes to the broader regenerative transition.
